preamble: Voicing of voiceless uvular fricative [χ] to voiced uvular fricative [ʁ], driven by assimilation to the voicing of surrounding sounds. Because place of articulation is unchanged, only voicing needs to be acquired — making this one of the more phonetically natural fricative alternations. The process is primarily allophonic: in languages with uvular fricatives, [χ] and [ʁ] typically form a voiced/voiceless allophonic pair conditioned by the voicing of neighboring segments, especially vowels. The clearest synchronic documentation comes from Standard German, where the uvular rhotic phoneme /R/ surfaces as voiced [ʁ] in onset position and before voiced segments, and as devoiced [χ] before voiceless consonants in some dialects.

examples.from: [χ] (devoiced allophone of /R/ before voiceless consonants, e.g. in Ruhr dialects)
examples.note: German /R/ is realized as the voiced uvular fricative [ʁ] (or approximant [ʁ̞]) when preceding vowels or voiced consonants. Before voiceless obstruents, it devoices to [χ] in certain dialects (notably the Ruhr). The χ→ʁ direction occurs whenever the phoneme /R/ moves from a devoicing to a voicing environment, for example in morphological alternations or in onset position.

In Tashlhiyt Berber, voiced fricatives including [ʁ] are fully or partially devoiced before voiceless obstruents (approaching [χ]). The reverse direction — assimilation to voiced surroundings — applies symmetrically: [χ] in a voiced context tends toward [ʁ]. This pattern is consistent with the general Tashlhiyt principle of voicing harmony across consonant clusters.

sources: Ridouane, R. (2014). Tashlhiyt Berber. Journal of the International Phonetic Association, 44(2), 207–221.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages. Blackwell.
